Notebook 02 — Cleaning, Basic Preprocessing & Pre-Feature-Engineering EDA¶

This notebook prepares the raw filtered data from Notebook 01 for feature engineering.

What we do here:

  • Load parquet from Notebook 01
  • Create target (default_ind)
  • Fix common dtype issues
  • Remove string noise in important numeric fields
  • Basic EDA (missingness, distributions, categorical frequencies, boxplots)
In [2]:
# Imports
import warnings; warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import os

# Show everything clearly
pd.set_option("display.max_rows", None)
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)

# Paths
base_path = r"C:\Users\deves\Documents\GitHub\portfolio\pdpit-lendingclub"
input_file = os.path.join(base_path, "data", "processed", "accepted_2015_to_2018_filtered.parquet")
eda_dir = os.path.join(base_path, "artifacts", "notebook02_eda")
os.makedirs(eda_dir, exist_ok=True)

print("Input file:", input_file)
print("EDA folder:", eda_dir)

Input file: C:\Users\deves\Documents\GitHub\portfolio\pdpit-lendingclub\data\processed\accepted_2015_to_2018_filtered.parquet
EDA folder: C:\Users\deves\Documents\GitHub\portfolio\pdpit-lendingclub\artifacts\notebook02_eda

Load dataset¶

We load the parquet created in Notebook 01.
We expect all raw LendingClub columns to be present (no column filtering happened previously).

In [4]:
df = pd.read_parquet(input_file)
print("Loaded shape:", df.shape)
display(df.head(3))

Create target variable: default_ind¶

Why: LendingClub uses many loan_status labels.
We need a binary PIT default flag:

  • 1 = Charged Off, Default, Late (>30 days)
  • 0 = Fully Paid, Current, In Grace Period

This is simple and consistent with industry PIT PD setup.

In [6]:
df["loan_status"].value_counts()
Out[6]:
loan_status
Current               866392
Fully Paid            702191
Charged Off           192060
Late (31-120 days)     21130
In Grace Period         8235
Late (16-30 days)       4276
Default                   39
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [7]:
# convert loan_status to lowercase for consistency
df["loan_status_clean"] = df["loan_status"].astype(str).str.lower()

# define bad statuses
bad_labels = ["charged off", "default", "late (31-120 days)"]

df["default_ind"] = df["loan_status_clean"].isin(bad_labels).astype("Int64")

df["default_ind"].value_counts(dropna=False)
Out[7]:
default_ind
0    1581094
1     213229
Name: count, dtype: Int64

Clean obvious string fields¶

Why: Some fields are numeric but stored as strings.
We convert them using simple replacements so Feature Engineering in Notebook 03 works cleanly.

Fix:

  • int_rate: "13.56%" → 13.56
  • term: "36 months" → 36
  • emp_length: "< 1 year", "10+ years" → numeric months
In [9]:
# interest rate like "13.56%"
df["int_rate"] = df["int_rate"].astype(str).str.replace("%", "", regex=False)
df["int_rate"] = pd.to_numeric(df["int_rate"], errors="coerce")
df["int_rate"].head()
Out[9]:
0    13.99
1    11.99
2    10.78
3    14.85
4    22.45
Name: int_rate, dtype: float64
In [10]:
# "36 months" → 36
df["term_clean"] = df["term"].astype(str).str.replace(" months", "", regex=False)
df["term_months"] = pd.to_numeric(df["term_clean"], errors="coerce")
df["term_months"].head()
Out[10]:
0    36
1    36
2    60
3    60
4    60
Name: term_months, dtype: int64
In [11]:
emp = df["emp_length"].astype(str)

emp = emp.str.replace("< 1 year", "0", regex=False)
emp = emp.str.replace("10+ years", "10", regex=False)
emp = emp.str.replace(" years", "", regex=False)
emp = emp.str.replace(" year", "", regex=False)
emp = emp.replace("n/a", np.nan)

df["emp_length_num"] = pd.to_numeric(emp, errors="coerce")
df["emp_length_num"].value_counts()
Out[11]:
emp_length_num
10.0    597932
2.0     162301
0.0     153720
3.0     144155
1.0     118781
5.0     108919
4.0     108581
6.0      76514
8.0      69517
7.0      66510
9.0      61504
Name: count, dtype: int64

Convert numeric-looking columns to numeric¶

Many columns (loan_amnt, annual_inc, dti, etc.) come as strings.
We convert them using pd.to_numeric.

In [13]:
num_cols_to_fix = [
    "loan_amnt","annual_inc","dti","delinq_2yrs","inq_last_6mths",
    "open_acc","total_acc","revol_bal","revol_util"
]

for c in num_cols_to_fix:
    df[c] = pd.to_numeric(df[c], errors="coerce")

Convert dates¶

Convert issue_d to pandas datetime to make time-based EDA in Notebook 04 work properly.

In [15]:
df["issue_d"] = pd.to_datetime(df["issue_d"], errors="coerce")
df["issue_year"] = df["issue_d"].dt.year
df["issue_month"] = df["issue_d"].dt.month

EDA Step 1 — Missingness¶

Why: Missingness drives imputation decisions and helps us understand feature reliability.

How: Simple .isna().sum() and percentage.

In [17]:
missing = df.isna().sum().to_frame("n_missing")
missing["pct_missing"] = missing["n_missing"] / len(df)
missing = missing.sort_values("pct_missing", ascending=False)

missing_file = os.path.join(eda_dir, "missingness_summary.csv")
missing.to_csv(missing_file)

print("Saved missingness summary:", missing_file)
display(missing)

Auto-remove very sparse columns (pct_missing > 30%)¶

What: identify features with more than 30% missing values (from the missing table already computed) and drop them from a working copy.

Why: fields with >30% missing are poor candidates to productionize (imputation risk, data pipeline fragility). We remove them now to simplify subsequent EDA and feature work. We keep a log of removed columns so this is auditable.

How: build remove_missing from missing['pct_missing'] > 0.30, save that list to artifacts/notebook02_eda/removed_missing_cols.csv, create df_trimmed = df.drop(columns = remove_missing), and show shape/head.

In [19]:
# Build list of columns to remove based on the missingness table computed earlier
# Assumes `missing` DataFrame exists (from earlier cell) with columns: ['feature','n_missing','pct_missing']
remove_missing = missing[missing['pct_missing'] > 0.30].index.tolist()

# Save list for audit
removed_file = os.path.join(eda_dir, "removed_missing_cols.csv")
pd.DataFrame({"removed_col": remove_missing}).to_csv(removed_file, index=False)
print("Saved list of auto-removed (high-missing) cols to:", removed_file)

# Show a quick sample of columns that will be removed (top 40)
print("Number of columns to be removed (pct_missing > 0.30):", len(remove_missing))
print("Sample of removed columns:")
display(remove_missing[:40])

# Create a trimmed working dataframe (do not overwrite original df yet)
df_trimmed = df.drop(columns=[c for c in remove_missing if c in df.columns], errors='ignore').copy()
print("Original df shape:", df.shape)
print("Trimmed df shape:", df_trimmed.shape)

# show a quick peek
display(df_trimmed.head(2))

Decision rules (applied now):

  • If pct_missing > 0.30 → column moved to removed_missing_cols.csv and dropped from df_trimmed (automatic).
  • Columns with 10% < pct_missing ≤ 30% remain for now and will be considered later during imputation or manual review.
  • The original df is preserved; df_trimmed is the working dataset for the next EDA/feature steps.
  • All removals are logged in removed_missing_cols.csv for validator review.
In [21]:
# Recompute describe() and missingness summary on the trimmed dataframe for downstream use
desc_trim = df_trimmed.describe(include='all').transpose()
desc_file = os.path.join(eda_dir, "describe_after_missing_removal.csv")
desc_trim.to_csv(desc_file)
print("Saved describe() of trimmed data to:", desc_file)

missing_trim = df_trimmed.isna().sum().to_frame("n_missing")
missing_trim["pct_missing"] = missing_trim["n_missing"] / len(df_trimmed)
missing_trim = missing_trim.sort_values("pct_missing", ascending=False)
missing_trim_file = os.path.join(eda_dir, "missingness_after_removal.csv")
missing_trim.to_csv(missing_trim_file)
print("Saved missingness summary after removal to:", missing_trim_file)

# Show top 20 still-missing columns after removal
display(missing_trim.head(20))

EDA Step 2 — Basic descriptive statistics¶

Why:¶

Descriptive statistics help us understand the scale, spread, and central tendencies of the remaining variables after removing columns with excessive missingness.
This step provides a simple snapshot of the dataset and is often the first place validators check for anomalies.

How:¶

We compute describe() for numeric columns and store the summary inside the EDA artifacts directory.
We also display the first few rows of the output to review.

In [23]:
# Basic descriptive statistics for numeric columns only
desc_num = df_trimmed.describe().transpose()

# Save the numeric summary for audit
desc_num_file = os.path.join(eda_dir, "describe_numeric.csv")
desc_num.to_csv(desc_num_file)

print("Saved numeric describe() to:", desc_num_file)

# Display a small part of the summary
display(desc_num.head(10))

Decision notes:¶

  • describe() is run only on numeric columns here because including object or category columns produces inconsistent summaries.
  • A full combined summary (numeric + categorical) will be created in upcoming steps.
  • No features are removed at this point; this step is only for inspection and documentation.

DType normalization before further EDA¶

What: Automatically detect and convert numeric-like and date-like columns in df_trimmed. Save lists of converted columns for audit.

Why: Notebook 01 loaded raw CSV as strings; treating numeric columns as categorical will break later EDA and modeling. Converting here keeps Notebook 02 focused on cleaning and pre-FE EDA.

How:

  1. For each object column, attempt pd.to_numeric(..., errors='coerce').
    • If at least 90% values convert (non-null after coercion), we cast column to numeric.
  2. For likely date columns (name contains common date tokens), attempt pd.to_datetime(..., errors='coerce').
    • If at least 90% parse, we cast column to datetime.
  3. Save lists of converted columns to CSVs in the EDA artifact folder.
In [26]:
# numeric conversion: simple loop, beginner style
converted_numeric = []
cols_to_check = [c for c in df_trimmed.columns if df_trimmed[c].dtype == "object"]

for c in cols_to_check:
    # try convert
    conv = pd.to_numeric(df_trimmed[c].str.replace("%","", regex=False), errors="coerce")
    non_null_ratio = conv.notna().sum() / len(conv)
    # decision threshold 90%
    if non_null_ratio >= 0.90:
        df_trimmed[c] = conv
        converted_numeric.append(c)
        print("Converted to numeric:", c, " non-null ratio:", round(non_null_ratio,3))

# save audit
num_conv_file = os.path.join(eda_dir, "converted_to_numeric.csv")
pd.DataFrame({"converted_to_numeric": converted_numeric}).to_csv(num_conv_file, index=False)
print("Saved numeric conversion list to:", num_conv_file)

Decision rule — numeric conversion

  • If >= 90% values convert to numeric → cast to numeric (automatic) and log.
  • If < 90% convert → leave as object (treat as categorical) for manual review later.
  • We stripped % before numeric coercion to handle percent strings like '13.56%'.
In [28]:
# date conversion candidates by name heuristics
date_candidates = [c for c in df_trimmed.columns if any(tok in c.lower() for tok in ["date","dt","issue_d","earliest_cr_line","last_pymnt","credit_pull","next_pymnt"])]

converted_dates = []
for c in date_candidates:
    # try parse
    parsed = pd.to_datetime(df_trimmed[c], errors="coerce", dayfirst=False)
    non_null_ratio = parsed.notna().sum() / len(parsed)
    if non_null_ratio >= 0.90:
        df_trimmed[c] = parsed
        converted_dates.append(c)
        print("Converted to datetime:", c, " non-null ratio:", round(non_null_ratio,3))

# save audit
date_conv_file = os.path.join(eda_dir, "converted_to_datetime.csv")
pd.DataFrame({"converted_to_datetime": converted_dates}).to_csv(date_conv_file, index=False)
print("Saved date conversion list to:", date_conv_file)

Converted to datetime: issue_d  non-null ratio: 1.0
Converted to datetime: dti  non-null ratio: 0.999
Converted to datetime: earliest_cr_line  non-null ratio: 1.0
Converted to datetime: last_pymnt_d  non-null ratio: 0.999
Converted to datetime: last_pymnt_amnt  non-null ratio: 1.0
Converted to datetime: last_credit_pull_d  non-null ratio: 1.0
Saved date conversion list to: C:\Users\deves\Documents\GitHub\portfolio\pdpit-lendingclub\artifacts\notebook02_eda\converted_to_datetime.csv

Decision rule — date conversion

  • If >= 90% of parsed values are valid datetimes → cast to datetime and log.
  • Otherwise leave as-is for manual inspection.
  • Typical date columns we attempt: issue_d, earliest_cr_line, last_pymnt_d, last_credit_pull_d, next_pymnt_d.
In [30]:
# recompute missingness and save
missing_trim = df_trimmed.isna().sum().to_frame("n_missing")
missing_trim["pct_missing"] = missing_trim["n_missing"] / len(df_trimmed)
missing_trim = missing_trim.sort_values("pct_missing", ascending=False)
missing_trim_file = os.path.join(eda_dir, "missingness_after_dtype_conversion.csv")
missing_trim.to_csv(missing_trim_file)
print("Saved updated missingness after dtype conversion to:", missing_trim_file)

# save a short summary of dtypes
dtypes_file = os.path.join(eda_dir, "dtypes_after_conversion.csv")
pd.DataFrame({"column": df_trimmed.columns, "dtype": [str(df_trimmed[c].dtype) for c in df_trimmed.columns]}).to_csv(dtypes_file, index=False)
print("Saved dtypes summary to:", dtypes_file)

# show quick previews
print("Top 10 still-missing columns after conversion:")
display(missing_trim.head(10))
print("Sample converted numeric columns:")
display(converted_numeric[:20])
print("Sample converted date columns:")
display(converted_dates[:20])

EDA Step 3 — Frequency tables for important categoricals¶

Why: After dtype normalization, we can reliably identify categorical columns and produce frequency tables. This helps find rare categories that may need grouping during Notebook 03.

How:

  • Identify categorical columns as those with dtype 'object' or pandas 'category'.
  • Save a CSV frequency table for each categorical column into the EDA artifacts folder.
  • Show a few sample frequency outputs for quick review.
In [32]:
# identify categorical columns (object or category dtype) after conversions
cat_cols = [c for c in df_trimmed.columns if (df_trimmed[c].dtype == "object" or str(df_trimmed[c].dtype).startswith("category"))]

print("Categorical columns found:", len(cat_cols))
# save list
pd.DataFrame({"categorical_cols": cat_cols}).to_csv(os.path.join(eda_dir, "categorical_columns_list.csv"), index=False)

# save freq tables
for c in cat_cols:
    vc = df_trimmed[c].value_counts(dropna=False).to_frame("count")
    vc["pct"] = vc["count"] / len(df_trimmed)
    fname = os.path.join(eda_dir, f"freq_{c}.csv")
    vc.to_csv(fname)
    # print small progress note (avoid heavy prints)
    if vc.shape[0] <= 10:
        print("Saved freq for:", c, "rows:", vc.shape[0])
    else:
        print("Saved freq for:", c, "top rows:", vc.head(3).to_dict()["count"])

Decision rule — frequency tables

  • No categories are merged or removed here.
  • If a categorical variable has too many unique values (e.g., > 500), we will consider frequency encoding or grouping in Notebook 03.
  • All frequency tables are saved for validator review.

EDA Step 4 — Correlation matrix (numeric)¶

Why: Now that numeric dtypes are fixed, compute correlation matrix for numeric features to understand redundancy before feature engineering.

How:

  • Use numeric columns from df_trimmed.
  • Save correlation matrix CSV and a small heatmap PNG.
In [35]:
from pandas.api import types as ptypes
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# identify numeric columns
num_cols = [c for c in df_trimmed.columns if ptypes.is_numeric_dtype(df_trimmed[c].dtype)]
print("Numeric columns count:", len(num_cols))
print("Sample numeric cols:", num_cols[:40])

if len(num_cols) >= 2:
    # compute correlation matrix
    corr = df_trimmed[num_cols].corr()

    # save correlation matrix
    corr_file = os.path.join(eda_dir, "correlation_matrix_after_dtype.csv")
    corr.to_csv(corr_file)
    print("Saved correlation matrix to:", corr_file)

    # pick up to 20 numeric columns for a readable heatmap
    topN = min(20, len(num_cols))
    top_cols = num_cols[:topN]

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))
    sns.heatmap(df_trimmed[top_cols].corr(), annot=False, cmap="RdBu", center=0, ax=ax)

    # save heatmap
    heatmap_file = os.path.join(eda_dir, "corr_top20_heatmap.png")
    fig.savefig(heatmap_file, bbox_inches="tight")
    print("Saved heatmap to:", heatmap_file)

    # show heatmap in notebook
    plt.show()

    # show a preview of correlation table
    display(corr.iloc[:10, :10])

else:
    print("Not enough numeric columns to compute correlation.")

EDA Step 5 — Histograms for numeric variables¶

Why:¶

Histograms show the shape of each numeric variable—skewness, heavy tails, unusual spikes, and the effects of earlier cleaning.
This helps identify variables that may require transformation in Notebook 03 (e.g., log transform, binning).

How:¶

  • Identify numeric columns after dtype conversion.
  • Loop through them one by one.
  • Make a simple matplotlib histogram.
  • Save each plot as a PNG and also display it in the notebook.
In [37]:
from pandas.api import types as ptypes
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# identify numeric columns
num_cols = [c for c in df_trimmed.columns if ptypes.is_numeric_dtype(df_trimmed[c].dtype)]

print("Number of numeric columns:", len(num_cols))

# folder for histogram images
hist_dir = os.path.join(eda_dir, "histograms")
os.makedirs(hist_dir, exist_ok=True)

for c in num_cols:
    plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
    df_trimmed[c].hist(bins=40)
    plt.title(f"Histogram: {c}")
    plt.xlabel(c)
    plt.ylabel("Frequency")
    
    # save file
    fname = os.path.join(hist_dir, f"hist_{c}.png")
    plt.savefig(fname, bbox_inches="tight")
    
    # show plot
    plt.show()

print("Saved all histograms to:", hist_dir)

Number of numeric columns: 73

Saved all histograms to: C:\Users\deves\Documents\GitHub\portfolio\pdpit-lendingclub\artifacts\notebook02_eda\histograms

Decision Notes:¶

  • Histograms are not used for selection directly; they help visually diagnose skewness and decide transformations in Notebook 03.
  • Features with extreme skewness may require log-transform or scaling later.
  • All histogram PNGs are saved for validator inspection.

EDA Step 6 — Boxplots (numeric variables)¶

Why:¶

Boxplots help visualize outliers and understand remaining spread after earlier cleaning.
They help confirm whether outlier handling was successful and whether certain features still have problematic tails.

How:¶

  • Use each numeric column individually (one plot per feature).
  • Save each boxplot in the EDA artifacts folder.
  • Display the plot inside the notebook.
In [40]:
# folder for boxplots
box_dir = os.path.join(eda_dir, "boxplots")
os.makedirs(box_dir, exist_ok=True)

for c in num_cols:
    plt.figure(figsize=(5,3))
    df_trimmed.boxplot(column=c)
    plt.title(f"Boxplot: {c}")
    
    fname = os.path.join(box_dir, f"box_{c}.png")
    plt.savefig(fname, bbox_inches="tight")
    plt.show()

print("Saved all boxplots to:", box_dir)

Saved all boxplots to: C:\Users\deves\Documents\GitHub\portfolio\pdpit-lendingclub\artifacts\notebook02_eda\boxplots

Decision Notes:¶

  • We do not remove or cap outliers here; that was handled earlier in Notebook 02.
  • These boxplots simply confirm the results and help validate earlier choices.
  • Features showing long tails will be considered for log-scaling or robust scaling in Notebook 03.

EDA Step 7 — Target variable distribution (default_ind)¶

Why:¶

Understanding the ratio of good vs. bad loans is crucial before building a PD model.
PD models are sensitive to class imbalance, and the distribution determines whether balancing techniques are required in Notebook 05.

How:¶

  • Simple value counts of default_ind.
  • Compute class ratio.
  • Save distribution as CSV.
  • Display a bar plot showing class imbalance.
In [43]:
# basic class distribution
target_counts = df_trimmed["default_ind"].value_counts(dropna=False)
target_pct = target_counts / len(df_trimmed)

target_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "count": target_counts,
    "pct": target_pct
})
display(target_df)

# save distribution
target_file = os.path.join(eda_dir, "target_distribution.csv")
target_df.to_csv(target_file)
print("Saved target distribution to:", target_file)

# bar plot
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))
target_counts.plot(kind="bar")
plt.title("Default Indicator Distribution")
plt.xlabel("default_ind")
plt.ylabel("Count")

target_plot = os.path.join(eda_dir, "target_distribution_plot.png")
plt.savefig(target_plot, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
count pct
default_ind
0 1581094 0.881165
1 213229 0.118835 
Saved target distribution to: C:\Users\deves\Documents\GitHub\portfolio\pdpit-lendingclub\artifacts\notebook02_eda\target_distribution.csv

Decision Notes:¶

  • This distribution will guide class-balancing decisions in Notebook 05.
  • If imbalance is large (e.g., bad rate < 5%), we may consider class weighting or stratified sampling later.
  • No modification to the dataset occurs at this step; this is purely diagnostic.
In [67]:
# Save the cleaned + trimmed dataset for Notebook 03
save_path = r"C:\Users\deves\Documents\GitHub\portfolio\pdpit-lendingclub\data\processed\accepted_2015_to_2018_filtered.parquet"
df_trimmed.to_parquet(save_path, index=False)

print("Saved cleaned/trimmed dataset to:")
print(save_path)
print("Shape:", df_trimmed.shape)
